title: 18 Tahsas — St. George & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "The eighteenth day of Tahsas is graced by the monthly feast of the Great Martyr George, the most beloved of all martyr-soldiers in the Ethiopian, Coptic, and wider Eastern Christian tradition, whose name in the Greek means 'farmer' or 'tiller of the earth' — a name that has seemed paradoxical for one celebrated as the supreme warrior-saint, yet the fathers see no contradiction, for it is the farmer who prepares the ground to receive the seed, and George's blood prepared the ground of many nations to receive the seed of the Gospel. George was a tribune of the Roman army who tore up the imperial edict of persecution, was subjected to an extraordinary series of torments that he survived by divine miracle, and was ultimately beheaded under Diocletian; the legends that gathered about his memory, including the great image of the dragon slain, declare in the language of sacred allegory his victory over the serpent of evil.

Saint Athanasius the Apostolic, Patriarch of Alexandria, whose monthly commemoration falls on this day, was the great fourth-century theologian whose name in the Greek means 'immortal' — and immortal proved to be the doctrine for which he labored without ceasing throughout a lifetime of exile and persecution: that the Son of God is fully divine, of one substance with the Father, and that this full divinity is the necessary condition for our salvation. Athanasius understood with perfect theological clarity that if the Son were merely a creature, however exalted, He could not truly unite us to the divine nature; only one who is truly God can make us truly partakers of the divine nature. His phrase 'He became man that we might become divine' is perhaps the most compressed and powerful statement of the theology of the Incarnation in all patristic literature.

Saint Elizabeth, the mother of John the Baptist, is commemorated on this day in the feast that celebrates her sanctity as the one who carried the Forerunner in her aged womb and who was the first human being to pronounce over the Virgin Mary the words that the Church has never ceased to repeat: 'Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb.' Elizabeth's name in the Hebrew means 'my God is my oath' or 'consecrated to God,' and her life was indeed a living covenant: barren for decades, she received the gift of the Forerunner in her old age, and her house became the site of the first encounter between the two children of the mystery — John leaping in the womb before the presence of the Lord who was already forming in Mary.

The theological seal of this eighteenth day of Tahsas is the mystery of preparation fully accomplished. George prepares the nations through the witness of his courage; Athanasius prepares the doctrinal understanding of the Church through the precision of his theology; Elizabeth prepares the welcoming community that receives the Mother of God with blessing. As the month approaches its climax, these three witnesses declare that the world has been sufficiently prepared, the doctrine sufficiently clarified, the welcome sufficiently warm — and that all that remains is for the Word Himself to appear in the fullness of the time appointed.
